acara_description: Conduct repeated chance experiments; compare predicted with observed results and explain the effect of sample size.

questions.stem: You flip a fair coin 100 times. Would you expect exactly 50 heads?
options: Yes, if the coin is fair
No — you always get more tails

default: Probability predicts the long-run pattern, not the exact count each time.
With 100 flips, results will be close to 50 but rarely exactly 50.
Random variation means results differ each time.

questions.worked_solution: The probability of 0.5 means approximately 50 heads in 100 flips, but exact results vary.

questions.stem: As the number of trials in an experiment increases, the experimental probability tends to:
options: Get further from the theoretical probability
Get closer to the theoretical probability

answer.correct: Get closer to the theoretical probability

default: This is called the Law of Large Numbers.
More trials → experimental probability converges on theoretical.
A small number of trials can have large variation.

questions.worked_solution: Law of Large Numbers: as trials increase, experimental probability approaches theoretical probability.

questions.stem: A die is rolled 60 times. How many times would you expect to roll a number greater than 4?

default: Numbers greater than 4 on a die: 5 and 6.
Expected = \(\frac{1}{3}\times60=20\).

questions.stem: Flip a fair coin 200 times. How many heads would you expect?

default: Expected heads = \(\frac{1}{2}\times200\).

questions.stem: You roll a die 20 times and get no 6s. What is the probability of getting a 6 on your next roll?

default: Each die roll is a separate event — past rolls do not affect future rolls.
P(6) is always \(\frac{1}{6}\) on a fair die.
The die has no memory.

questions.worked_solution: Each roll is independent. P(6) = \(\frac{1}{6}\) regardless of previous results.

questions.stem: In 50 trials you observe 18 successes. What is the experimental probability of success?

default: Experimental probability = favourable outcomes ÷ total trials.
Simplify if needed: \(\frac{9}{25}=0.36\).

questions.stem: Which experiment gives the most reliable probability estimate?

default: More trials means less random variation.
The experimental probability gets closer to theoretical with more trials.
1000 trials gives the most reliable estimate.

questions.worked_solution: More trials → Law of Large Numbers → experimental probability closer to theoretical.

questions.stem: A spinner has 4 equal sections. In 40 spins, how many times would you expect it to land on one particular section?

default: P(one section) = \(\frac{1}{4}\).
Expected = \(\frac{1}{4}\times40\).

questions.stem: You observe 65 heads in 100 coin flips. What is the most reasonable conclusion?
options: More trials are needed to draw a reliable conclusion
It is impossible to get 65 heads from a fair coin

answer.correct: More trials are needed to draw a reliable conclusion

default: 65 heads in 100 could happen by chance even with a fair coin.
The probability of this is not extremely low.
More trials would help determine if there is genuine bias.

questions.worked_solution: 65/100 is somewhat unusual but not impossible for a fair coin. More trials are needed.

questions.stem: The difference between experimental and theoretical probability tends to...
options: increase as more trials are conducted
decrease as more trials are conducted

answer.correct: decrease as more trials are conducted

default: The Law of Large Numbers explains this.
More trials → experimental probability converges on theoretical.
The gap decreases (doesn't necessarily reach zero).

questions.worked_solution: More trials → experimental probability gets closer to theoretical probability (Law of Large Numbers).
